---
title: "Expanding the UK DECC network: new GEMMA instrumentation at Jodrell Bank and Invergowrie"
date: 2026-05-22
author:
  - name: "Joe Pitt"
    affiliation: "University of Bristol"
  - name: "Kieran Stanley"
    affiliation: "University of Bristol"
  - name: "Simon O'Doherty"
    affiliation: "University of Bristol"
  - name: "Grant Foster"
    affiliation: "UEA"
categories:
  - network expansion
  - DECC
  - halocarbons
  - methane
  - carbon dioxide
image: /assets/case_study_network_expansion-fig1.png
description: >
  GEMMA has expanded the UK DECC atmospheric monitoring network with two new sites:
  a Picarro CO2/CH4 analyser at Jodrell Bank and a Medusa GCMS system at Invergowrie.
  The expansion strengthens devolved-administration emissions estimates and
  substantially improves constraints on UK halocarbon emissions.
---

::: {.pdf-download-bar}
**Download this page as a PDF policy brief:**
[Download PDF](decc-network-expansion.pdf){.btn .btn-sm .btn-primary}
:::

::: {.highlight-meta}
**Authors:** Joe Pitt, Kieran Stanley, Simon O'Doherty (University of Bristol), and Grant Foster (UEA) \
**Published:** 22 May 2026 \
**Related network information:** [University of Bristol ACRG DECC network page](https://www.bristol.ac.uk/chemistry/research/acrg/current/decc.html)
:::

## Why this matters

The UK DECC (Deriving Emissions linked to Climate Change) network is central to
independent, atmospheric verification of greenhouse gas emissions across the UK and
Ireland.

GEMMA has now equipped two additional sites with new instrumentation, increasing
network coverage and analytical capability across the UK:

- **Jodrell Bank (Cheshire):** a new **Picarro** system measuring **CO2** and **CH4**
- **Invergowrie (Scotland):** a new **Medusa GCMS** system measuring more than 40
  halogenated ozone-depleting substances and greenhouse gases

Together, these upgrades provide stronger observational constraints for UK emissions
estimation, especially outside southern England and for gases that are difficult to
quantify from bottom-up inventories alone. They also provide a stronger evidence base
for policy development in each devolved administration.

![Map of the UK and Ireland DECC network showing monitoring sites and the new GEMMA instrumentation at Jodrell Bank and Invergowrie.](/assets/case_study_network_expansion-fig1.png){fig-alt="Map of DECC network expansion highlighting Jodrell Bank and Invergowrie."}

## What was installed

### Jodrell Bank: continuous CO2 and CH4 measurements

At Jodrell Bank, GEMMA installed a **Picarro cavity ring-down spectroscopy analyser**
for high-frequency CO2 and CH4 observations. This site adds valuable regional coverage
for major greenhouse gases and helps improve source attribution across England, Wales,
and Northern Ireland when combined with other UK and Irish stations.

### Invergowrie: expanded halocarbon capability with Medusa GCMS

At Invergowrie, GEMMA installed a **Medusa GCMS** system capable of measuring over
40 halogenated gases, including:

- HFCs
- CFCs
- HCFCs
- SF6
- NF3
- SO2F2

These species are highly climate-relevant, and many are emitted from diffuse or
sector-specific activities where inventory uncertainty can be large.

### Weybourne: better integration through unified data evaluation

GEMMA is also improving integration of the **Weybourne** station (run by the
National Centre for Atmospheric Science, NCAS/UEA) with the DECC network by
unifying data evaluation processes across sites.

This harmonisation improves comparability between stations, supports more consistent
quality control, and makes network-wide interpretation more robust for policy use.

## Scientific and policy impact

The network expansion is designed to deliver two major outcomes.

1. **More accurate emissions estimates for devolved administrations**

Improved spatial coverage strengthens top-down constraints for emissions from
England, Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland individually, not only for the UK
total.

2. **Dramatically improved quantification of UK halocarbon emissions**

With this deployment, the UK and Ireland now operate **four Medusa systems**
(including Mace Head, Tacolneston, and Ridge Hill), making them the most intensively
monitored countries in the world for these gases.

This level of coverage supports faster detection of changing emission patterns,
improved inventory verification, and stronger evidence for climate and air-quality
policy.

## What comes next

As data from the new instruments accumulate, GEMMA will integrate them into inverse
modelling frameworks to:

- tighten uncertainty ranges on national and devolved-administration emissions
- improve sectoral interpretation of halocarbon trends
- provide robust, independent evidence to support UK reporting and mitigation policy
